title: Park Service, family strike Katmai deal
summary: Park Service, family strike Katmai deal By NATALIE PHILLIPS Daily News reporter Melgenak's family land in relation to Katmai National Park. After a 25-year legal battle followed by a summer of intense negotiation, the National Park Service has struck a deal with a Native family that owns a key piece of bear viewing land in the heart of Katmai National Park. The deal involves a land swap, lets the family keep a portion of their original land and awards them $4.4 million. The agreement also requires the Park Service to construct a family memorial at the site. "The fact that we were forced to do it, I can't be happy about that," said Ralph Angasan Sr., a descendant of Palakia Melgenak, an Aleut matriarch who filed the original claim to the land. "The money is always something, but the land is…

title: Sea may not suit salmon
summary: Sea may not suit salmon Experts debate ideas on cause of low runs By TOM KIZZIA Daily News reporter Something was wrong with the king salmon on the Yukon River this year, and it wasn't just that there were so few of them. In a summer with unexpectedly weak salmon runs across Alaska, the Yukon River saw the worst return since statehood. Beyond that, the few kings that made it back appeared battered by life at sea. Their bodies were unusually small compared with their head sizes, according to state biologists. Sea lampreys and a muscle fungus had attacked their flesh. These were signs that stress from the marine environment - not foreign fleet interception or marine mammal predation - was responsible for the poor returns, said Tom Kron, a regional supervisor for the state Department of Fish …

title: Rescue saves 3 climbers
summary: Rescue saves 3 climbers 2 stranded Brits still on McKinley By RACHEL D'ORO Daily News reporter Rescuers plucked three injured climbers Sunday night from the 14,200-foot level of Mount McKinley, but continuing clouds and high winds thwarted efforts to reach two British military climbers stranded for three days near the peak. An Army Chinook helicopter from Fort Wainwright picked up one American and two British climbers at about 8:15 p.m., said Jane Tranel, spokeswoman for Denali National Park and Preserve. The three men were flown to Talkeetna, where they took a Cessna Conquest to Alaska Regional Hospital, arriving about 10:15 p.m. Sunday. The National Park Service launched a LAMA high-altitude helicopter to wait at the 14,000-foot level for a break in the weather to look for the two Britis…

title: Copyright © 1998 The Anchorage Daily News
summary: Storm curses rescue Tired crews fight Denali snow, wind By ELIZABETH MANNING Daily News reporter Rescuers fought fatigue and miserable weather Saturday on Mount McKinley trying to reach two climbers marooned near the summit and four other injured climbers waiting in a camp built at 14,200 feet. Two climbing teams, one a group of nine British soldiers and the other a pair of Anchorage climbers, were involved in three separate falls. Members of the British group fell twice, once late Thursday and once Friday. The Americans fell early Friday and were discovered when members of the British team, seeking help for their own injuries, stumbled upon them. It's not known what caused the accidents, which occurred on and near a slick chute known as the Orient Express. The injured climber in the worst…

title: A new friend with every step
summary: A new friend with every step 'Japanese Caribou' walks to know the 'north place' By PETER PORCO Daily News reporter The "Japanese Caribou," a young man who aspires to the profession of adventurer, is crossing the Brooks Range on his 850-mile walk from Anchorage to Prudhoe Bay. Masatoshi Kuriaki, who climbed to the top of Mount McKinley by himself late last winter, was reported camping just south of Atigun Pass on Friday night. That's the highest highway summit in Alaska, 4,800 feet elevation. He had walked 675 miles to get there, pulling his cart up the highway and making inroads into people's hearts along the way. "My tripping speed very slow, but I have very good experience for me, so I have many many friend, local people and tourists also," Kuriaki said last weekend by telephone from Col…

title: Climbing past his demons
summary: Climbing past his demons Crash victim returns to Denali By TIM MURRAY Daily News reporter Map of climbers' route up McKinley. Camped almost three miles high on Mount McKinley, Ed Hommer stirs in his sleep, thrashing and breathing so fitfully he rouses his exhausted tent mate from a deep slumber. Bad dream at 14,000 feet. Hundreds of times over the years the same nightmare has invaded Hommer's sleep. But in the thin air of North America's highest peak it seems more raw, more real. The dream is a cruel flashback to a tragic December day when a plane crash, a blizzard and the deaths of two men fractured Hommer's life in unthinkable ways. I'm back at the crash site, back inside the airplane wreckage, rocking back and forth and trying to stay warm. It's cold and it's dark, and I'm fighting for …

title: Cold spell shows no sign of letting up
summary: Cold spell shows no sign of letting up By ELIZABETH MANNING Daily News reporter Much of Alaska remained in the icy grip of a deep freeze Sunday, with some areas seeing their coldest temperatures in 10 years. The coldest spot was the Athabaskan village of Huslia, 290 miles west of Fairbanks, where the temperature dipped to 67 below. The weather in Huslia was cold enough to thicken fuel oil and break heaters, but residents took the freeze in stride. "Everybody's snug," Huslia resident Maudy Sommers said. "All the young guys are checking on the elders to make sure everything's fine." In Cantwell, near Denali National Park and Preserve, the temperature reached 35 below, but winds gusting to 55 mph made it feel like 107 below. Again, residents were unfazed. "We just add another cover, stay on t…

title: Carbon monoxide poisonings rise
summary: Carbon monoxide poisonings rise The Associated Press KENAI - The number of carbon monoxide poisonings on the Kenai Peninsula is up sharply this winter, and emergency officials worry that continuing cold weather will push the total even higher. So far this year, 36 cases of carbon monoxide poisoning have been reported. That's up from 23 cases during the winters of 1996 and 1997. Early last year, a family of four in Nikiski died of carbon monoxide poisoning. There have been no carbon monoxide-related fatalities so far this winter. "We've had two really close calls already this year and have sent two families to the hyperbaric chamber in Anchorage," said Central Emergency Services Fire Chief Len Malmquist. The hyperbaric chamber treats victims of exposure with oxygen and high pressure, which …

title: Human migration wasn't affected
summary: Human migration wasn't affected The Associated Press Though the Bering Strait is best known as the route for human migration between Eurasia and North America, the new findings about its early opening have no bearing on man's arrival in North America. Modern humans are believed to have reached North America during the last ice age, possibly as late as 30,000 years ago. The land bridge, which despite its name wasn't narrow - it was about 1,000 miles wide - repeatedly opened and closed over the millennia. It last closed 11,000 years ago, when meltwater at the end of the ice age dramatically raised sea levels worldwide. "This is one more piece of a big jigsaw puzzle that helps us estimate the migrations of animals in either direction," said David Hopkins, a retired distinguished professor of …

title: Ski for Women beats deep chill with warm spirit
summary: Ski for Women beats deep chill with warm spirit By J.R. RARDON Daily News reporter While many Anchorage residents enjoyed the big game Sunday, several hundred Alaska women opted for the big chill. Thumbing its nose at wind, snow and temperatures that dipped to 15 below, the third annual Ski for Women turned Super Bowl Sunday into a celebration of sisterhood at Kincaid Park. While the football game featured combatants, the Ski for Women had camaraderie. While the players wore helmets and cleats, the skiers wore prom dresses and tuxedos. And while the football game had the Dirty Bird, the Ski for Women had the purple birds. "Actually, the feathers are kind of warm," said Lupe Marroquin, who joined with ski partner Ruth Carter to wear outfits of bright purple feathers from head to skis. While…

title: Guinea pigs bring home the bacon
summary: Guinea pigs bring home the bacon Woman breeds prize critters By S.J. KOMARNITSKY Daily News Mat-Su Bureau PALMER - Peg Smith is the "mother" of several national champions. Not skiers or baseball players or runners but furry, lovable creatures with scrunched-up noses, lustrous coats and a penchant for carrots and bits of corn. Smith's prize-winners are guinea pigs. Her trailer on 15 acres off Caribou Street is home to more than 80 of these pocket-size pets. A few years ago she had 250. She cut back partly at her husband's request. But even today, the guinea pigs' metal cages, lined with fluffy pine shavings, fill up an entire room of her home. A second room is dedicated to awards, binders of pedigree literature and guinea pig knickknacks like calendars, a guinea pig necklace and a tick-tack…

title: Suspect's ID may be lead in theft string
summary: Suspect's ID may be lead in theft string The Associated Press FAIRBANKS - A Fairbanks jewelry thief forgot one small thing as he dashed from a shopping mall with a $5,000 ring. He left his photo identification card with the sales clerk. "It is pretty funny," Fairbanks Police officer David Elzey said. "I'm laughing about it myself." The thief's apparent blunder may help solve a string of jewelry store robberies that have plagued Fairbanks since mid-December. All five robberies, including Saturday's, followed a similar pattern. A man would enter a store, try on an expensive watch or piece of jewelry and then run from the store with it. The suspect stole about $10,000 in merchandise using the simple ruse. Because of the string of thefts, police instructed jewelry store clerks to get identific…

title: Inlet's future a concern
summary: Inlet's future a concern West side pushes resource safeguards The Associated Press KENAI - Few people venture to the western shore of Cook Inlet. But those who live in the sparsely populated area say it is suffering from steep declines in salmon populations and wildlife. Those declines may herald serious problems with the ecosystem, say residents of Chinitna Bay, near Mount Iliamna. They want steps taken to study and safeguard the resources. "Our situation in Chinitna Bay is a red flag for the whole west side," said Mary Haeg. Haeg and her husband, Bob, have lived near the remote bay since 1976. In recent years they have seen changes there that have alarmed them. The Haegs report an upsurge in human traffic in the area, a decline in wildlife and the virtual disappearance of salmon over the…

title: BP puts tax cut on table
summary: BP puts tax cut on table But lawmakers say budget comes first By ROBERT KOWALSKI Daily News Juneau Bureau JUNEAU - The president of the state's largest oil producer, BP Exploration (Alaska) Inc., told legislators on Thursday that the company wants the state to consider decreasing oil taxes and tying them more directly to company profits. The idea quickly got the cold shoulder from top state leaders, who said Alaska government is first concentrating on balancing its own budget, hit hard in recent months by low world oil prices. BP and other companies have cut their Alaska work forces and say they're spending less on development here because of low prices. BP's Alaska president, Richard Campbell, said the company wants to begin discussions with state leaders to change the tax structure to be…

title: It's the fish, stupid
summary: It's the fish, stupid Salmon linked to economy By JON LITTLE Daily News Peninsula Bureau SOLDOTNA - Salmon that swim into Cook Inlet this summer may not know it, but their spawning urge has been linked to Anchorage's hotel boom and even to the price of bread. Southcentral Alaska is so wrapped up in salmon fishing that major changes in regional fisheries might send a shock wave through the economy, tourism and cargo officials told the state Board of Fisheries Wednesday at its meeting here. "The total economic package is so huge that decisions made here could easily affect the way the economy could go," said Bill Elander, president of the Anchorage Convention and Visitors Bureau. In a meeting scheduled to last the next two weeks, the board will decide on more than 200 proposals aimed at chan…

title: Stevens extends Denali panel's numbers, role
summary: Stevens extends Denali panel's numbers, role By DAVID WHITNEY Daily News Washington Bureau WASHINGTON - Alaska Sen. Ted Stevens has expanded his proposed Denali Commission and broadened its role to look beyond just rural Alaska in addressing entrenched problems of unemployment and inadequate local services. Stevens authorized the commission in a 1999 Senate spending bill last week that earmarked $20 million for the first of its five years of operation. Changes were made to the final text late Friday, however. The most prominent change expands the new commission from three to five members, while both the governor and the Alaska Federation of Natives lose designated seats. Under the new structure, the governor would appoint a member in "consultation" with the AFN. New seats were added for th…

title: Legal experts wary of tax
summary: Legal experts wary of tax By ROBERT KOWALSKI Daily News Juneau Bureau Knowles income tax proposal JUNEAU -Gov. Tony Knowles proposed income tax may be unconstitutional because of the way it discriminates between Alaska residents and nonresidents, prominent Alaska legal experts said this week. Even Attorney General Bruce Botelho acknowledged Wednesday there's no guarantee the proposal would survive a constitutional court challenge. "The courts could go either way," he said. "I think there would be no dispute among lawyers who are familiar with the issues that that is an issue in controversy, and I don't think it's a slam-dunk (in) either direction," Botelho said. Knowles' proposed tax would raise about $350 million a year to help balance the state budget. The tax would apply to residents an…

title: Biologists urge Kenai rules shift
summary: Biologists urge Kenai rules shift Plan alters king goals By JON LITTLE Daily News Peninsula Bureau SOLDOTNA - Anglers trolling for big Kenai River king salmon next summer may fish under a simpler, more predictable, set of rules. Instead of requiring the state to impose unpopular catch-and-release restrictions if the early king run appears to be falling short of 9,200 spawners, the proposed new rules would let anglers continue fishing as long as the run is within a broad range: 7,200 and 14,400 spawners. The same approach would apply to the river's late run of kings. "We're not shooting for the midpoint, we're shooting for the range," said Doug McBride, Fish and Game's regional sportfish director. The suggestion by state biologists to revamp king spawning goals is just one of 200 proposals …

title: Environmentalists appeal Cook Inlet leases
summary: Environmentalists appeal Cook Inlet leases The Associated Press KENAI - A number of environmental groups have appealed the state's plans for an oil and gas lease sale in April that would cover more than 4 million acres in the Cook Inlet region. The state Department of Natural Resources last month approved the Cook Inlet areawide lease sale after determining the sale was in Alaska's best interest. The sale includes subsurface rights on land extending from Wasilla to Anchor Point, as well as offshore tracts and property on the Inlet's west side. "The main point is that the state has failed to look at the cumulative effects of such a large sale," said Bob Shavelson of the Homer environmental group Cook Inlet Keeper, which joined an appeal filed by Trustees for Alaska. Trustees for Alaska, an …

title: 10 years later, wildlife yet to rebound from Exxon spill
summary: 10 years later, wildlife yet to rebound from Exxon spill By MAUREEN CLARK The Associated Press Ten years after the tanker Exxon Valdez spilled 11 million gallons of crude oil into Prince William Sound, only two of the nearly two dozen species hurt by the disastrous spill are fully recovered, according to the state-federal panel overseeing restoration of the spill area. The Exxon Valdez Oil Spill Trustee Council on Tuesday updated the official status of the birds, fish and marine mammals hurt by the spill. The council added river otters to its list of species considered recovered. They join bald eagles, which were declared recovered 21/2 years ago. The March 24, 1989, spill fouled 1,300 miles of shoreline and killed hundreds of thousands of seabirds and thousands of otters, seals and other …

title: Copyright © 1999 The Anchorage Daily News
summary: The Real Deal By VAN WILLIAMS Daily News reporter JUNEAU - Carlos Boozer stretches out in a swivel chair he's much too large for at his family's home, intently watching a college hoops game on TV. His long frame dwarfs the brown chair, with his knees extending higher than the arm rest. Boozer's brown eyes seldom drift from the direction of the screen until a player he knows is shown shooting free throws. Boozer turns to his father. "I dunked on him last summer, Dad," he said, before turning his attention back to the tube. He might as well have been watching his future. Next year, Boozer will be one of those college players on TV. The only question remaining is which college uniform he'll wear. Boozer's imposing 6-foot-9, 246-pound frame is no doubt a giant reason he has achieved so much su…

title: Denali snowmobile rule blasted
summary: Denali snowmobile rule blasted By S.J. KOMARNITSKY Daily News Mat-Su Bureau Denali National Park and Preserve PALMER - Most of the heart of Denali National Park and Preserve will remain off-limits to snowmachines, the National Park Service said Thursday, but riders can continue to cruise a 25-mile loop near Cantwell that has become popular in recent years. The decision was immediately blasted by both the Wilderness Society, which wants the park closed to motorized access, and by the Alaska State Snowmobile Association, which wants it opened to riders. Both groups said the order violates the Alaska National Interest Lands Conservation Act - the 1980 federal law that tripled the size of the park. "It's pathetic," said Joe Gauna of the snowmachine group. He said the association will file a la…

title: Unalaska digs out from storm
summary: Unalaska digs out from storm 21 inches of snow falls in big weekend blizzard By JIM PAULIN Daily News correspondent UNALASKA - Unalaska was still digging out Monday from a blustery weekend blizzard that dropped more than a foot of snow and drifts of up to 6 feet on this Aleutian Island community. "It's been many a year since we've had a dumping like that," said Unalaska Port Director Jim Severns. "We used to get them like that fairly often in the 1970s." The blizzard, backed by winds gusting to 100 miles an hour, blew in from the west, an unusual direction for cold air to come from. Westerly winds usually bring rain, Severns said. Severns said he spent three hours Monday morning shoveling out his house and vehicle. It took him an hour just to dig through a 6-foot snowdrift blocking his fro…

title: Wolves get their lice medication
summary: Wolves get their lice medication Biologists treat 27 of 28, say no killing necessary By S.J. KOMARNITSKY Daily News Mat-Su Bureau Mat-Su wolf packs PALMER - Biologists trying to stop an outbreak of lice in Matanuska-Susitna-area wolves have treated all but one of the 28 wolves in three infested packs, state wildlife officials said Monday. One of the packs roams near Talkeetna. Another lives near Montana Creek, and the third ranges near the Deshka River. Biologists hope to catch the last wolf - part of the five-member Montana Creek pack - later this week, said Jeff Hughes, a regional Fish and Game Department supervisor. That animal wasn't with the pack this weekend, he said. Biologists are waiting for better flying and tracking weather before they try to find it again. The treatment include…

title: Both chains place more faith in local judgment
summary: Both chains place more faith in local judgment By EVE ROSE Daily News reporter Safeway and Carrs officials want the state to evaluate their merger for potential antitrust violations because they say the state understands the Alaska market better. Why is that important to them? Antitrust law is designed to protect consumers when one competitor buys another. The question regulators have to answer is whether consumers will be hurt by the deal. The main issue they look at is how the deal will affect prices. For example, if there are just two stores in a town selling lawn mowers and one buys the other, the remaining store would have a monopoly and would be in a position to raise prices. If a deal violates antitrust laws, the government can block the sale completely or require the purchaser to s…

title: Cold forces rescue of SEALS
summary: Cold forces rescue of SEALS By CRAIG MEDRED Daily News outdoors editor See Related File Hostile Alaska weather beat up one of the U.S. military's premier combat units Wednesday, forcing its members to cry uncle. Nineteen U.S. Navy SEALS - members of an elite organization the Pentagon touts as "the most feared special forces unit in the world" - were airlifted out of Ship Creek Valley just south of Arctic Valley Ski Area after radioing the Rescue Coordination Center at nearby Elmendorf Air Force Base. Eleven of the SEALS suffered frostbite. Of those, seven were examined at the Elmendorf hospital and released, an Air Force spokeswoman said. Three were held overnight and let go Thursday. One remains hospitalized. The Air Force declined to say what injuries the SEALS suffered, but SEALS spokes…

title: Senate attempts to limit Knowles' spending power
summary: Senate attempts to limit Knowles' spending power By PAUL QUEARY The Associated Press JUNEAU - The first bill passed by the Alaska Senate this year aims to reduce the governor's power to spend more money than the Legislature approves in the annual budget. When the Legislature is not in session, Gov. Tony Knowles can spend money from some sources without the approval of the Legislative Budget and Audit Committee, a panel of lawmakers. All he has to do is wait 45 days after the panel turns him down. "In the 20 years that I've been here, no governor has so abused it as this governor," Sen. Rick Halford, R-Chugiak, said before the measure passed 14-5 Wednesday in a party-line vote. The bill, Senate Bill 28, would force the administration to wait until 30 days into the next regular session of th…

title: Beluga's decline won't halt Inlet lease sale
summary: Beluga's decline won't halt Inlet lease sale By JON LITTLE Daily News Peninsula Bureau SOLDOTNA - A state oil and gas lease sale spanning the Cook Inlet basin is on track for April, despite calls to delete key tracts near river mouths that are important to the region's declining population of beluga whales. The number of Cook Inlet belugas dropped from an estimated high of 1,000 earlier this decade to a 1998 count of 347, leading the National Marine Fisheries Service to explore whether the animals qualify for federal protection. The federal agency told the state in a letter last summer that the population has reached the point that belugas need extra care. Steven Pennoyer, Alaska administrator for the agency, asked the state to withdraw oil and gas tracts in areas seen as critical to belug…

title: Salmon funding floated
summary: Salmon funding floated Clinton proposes $100 million in help By FRANK CLIFFORD Los Angeles Times In a major initiative to revive vanishing wild salmon stock, President Clinton called Wednesday for spending $100 million to help West Coast states and Indian tribes improve water quality and repair habitat in rivers and streams used by the endangered fish. "We want to help you bring the salmon back," said Clinton as he and Vice President Al Gore detailed the proposal on a conference call to the governors of Alaska, Washington and Oregon and the California resources secretary. The administration would allow the money to be used for a broad range of salmon-protection activities, from buying streamside land to mapping habitats. Exactly how much flexibility state and local governments should have …

title: Camp workers gain pay
summary: Camp workers gain pay Ruling may mean higher user fees By JOHN HUGHES The Associated Press WASHINGTON - Hundreds of minimum-wage workers cleaning toilets, picking up trash and greeting vacationers at Forest Service campgrounds are about to get raises of between $1 to $5 an hour. The pay increases follow Labor Department rejection of a Forest Service request for exemption from the 1965 McNamara-O'Hara Service Contract Act, which requires federal contractors to pay prevailing union-level wages to service workers. Their employers aren't happy, and chances are that many campers - overall, the sites get as many as 60 million visits a year - won't be either. Companies that run most of the Forest Service's 4,000 or so campgrounds, mostly in the West, say they may have to boost overnight camping f…

title: Copyright © 1999 The Anchorage Daily News
summary: Great ball of fire Flash in sky likely exploding meteor By ELIZABETH MANNING Daily News reporter A brilliant flash and earthshaking boom noticed by thousands of Alaskans late Friday were likely a meteor exploding in Earth's atmosphere, scientists said Saturday. Donald Martins, an astronomy professor at the University of Alaska Anchorage, wasn't lucky enough to see the object that streaked blue, green and red across the Southcentral Alaska sky. But based on witness accounts, Martins and other scientists believe the flash came from a meteor or comet fragment - probably the size of a pumpkin - that exploded about 50 miles above Earth's surface. "That's almost certainly what it was," Martins said. "They aren't uncommon. But it is very rare to have one explode and hear it." Dozens of people pho…

title: Railroad, doctor make Brandy smile
summary: Railroad, doctor make Brandy smile Readers fulfill wishes By SONYA SENKOWSKY Daily News reporter When 12-year-old Katie Koropp read about a girl from Whittier too embarrassed to smile because of her crooked teeth, she knew just the man for the job: her dad. After his daughter drew his attention to 13-year-old Brandy Bender's holiday wish, said orthodontist Michael Koropp, he couldn't resist helping. Brandy's story, which told of her triumph over dyslexia and her mother's desire to give her a smile, was one of 14 featured in the Book of Dreams, a special Daily News supplement published Nov. 22. The article asked readers for help to pay the $1,200 down payment required to start conservative treatment of Brandy's teeth - enough to at least improve her smile. Brandy's mother, Terry, agreed to …

title: Airman accused of being hacker
summary: Airman accused of being hacker By NATALIE PHILLIPS Daily News reporter An Elmendorf airman was indicted by a federal grand jury Wednesday on charges that he hacked into two U.S. Air Force computers at the base. A hacker was detected on Nov. 12 by what the Air Force calls an "intrusion detection system." The system immediately alerted the Air Force Computer Emergency Response Team. An emergency team analyst then monitored the hacking as it occurred, according to the indictment. "The analyst notified personnel at Elmendorf, who verified the attack as an unauthorized intrusion," according to the indictment. Using a "numeric identifier" assigned to every computer that logs onto the Internet, law officials were able to trace the "attack" to a local Internet service provider and eventually to th…

title: Experts fear video games breed violence
summary: Experts fear video games breed violence By STEVE FAINARU The Boston Globe Last in a series Don Ramsey guided the mouse across the pad. "This is where I think he got his strategy," he said. The mouse controlled a hand. The hand held a gun as it negotiated a maze of animated carnage: blood-drenched corpses, mutants obliterated and the ultimate killing machine, the B.F.G. 9000, which stands for Big Fragging Gun. Ramsey believes his son, Evan, was emulating the popular computer game "Doom" when he raced through the corridors of Bethel Regional High with a shotgun last year. Evan ducked behind corners and blew holes in the ceiling. He killed a 15-year-old sophomore, then killed the principal by firing into his shoulder from a few feet away. Obviously, most children who use fantasy guns never ki…

title: See related story from:
summary: Fund debate flames Knowles plan divides sides By ROBERT KOWALSKI Daily News Juneau Bureau JUNEAU - Gov. Tony Knowles' proposal to tap the Alaska Permanent Fund for the first time to help pay for state government has drawn some praise, some reservations and some blunt criticism from current and past managers of the fund. Present Permanent Fund trustees say the proposal to use the fund's stock market gains to balance the state budget is well-timed and appropriate. "I think it's consistent with the amendment and the original purpose," said current trustee Clark Gruening, who helped draft the state constitutional amendment that voters approved to set up the fund in 1976. But others like Elmer Rasmuson, the fund's first chairman, said it would be unwise to let the Legislature make investment de…

title: Mood somber at BP building
summary: Mood somber at BP building By LIZ RUSKIN Daily News reporter A funereal atmosphere settled over the BP Exploration (Alaska) Inc. headquarters, where company president Richard Campbell announced in the morning that 600 workers - and nearly half the workers in the Midtown building - would soon be laid off. Some learned their fate Monday. Some were still waiting for the word. Others, given the bad news days ago, have already moved beyond grief to acceptance. And like mourners at a funeral, there were those who said it was simply their time to go and, God willing, they were headed for a better place. Peter Burke, an Englishman who has been working as a reservoir engineer, received one of the pink slips, after 24 years with the company. He described the mood in the building as grim. "You accept…

title: Denali options explored
summary: Denali options explored Panel examines park development S.J. KOMARNITSKY Daily News Mat-Su Bureau PALMER - Plans to develop the south side of Denali National Park have tried to gain footing for almost 30 years. But from then-Sen. Mike Gravel's dream of a domed city rising near Mount McKinley to more recent proposals for a visitor center overlooking the Tokositna River, they have shared the same fate. They've been too controversial to become reality. In a new attempt, a 12-member "consultation committee" appointed by the governor is searching for a compromise in a thicket of competing values and visions. They are finding the job as difficult as those who tried before. The group, which includes local residents and miners as well as representatives of area Native corporations, state environme…

title: Donations made for paralyzed Quest musher
summary: Donations made for paralyzed Quest musher The Associated Press FAIRBANKS - A special edition Beanie Baby husky is helping pull a paralyzed Yukon Quest musher toward recovery. So far, about $40,000 in Canadian money (about $26,100 U.S.) has been raised for Peter Zimmerman's medical bills and other costs through sales of the special Beanie Baby by the Canadian Quest organization. And Nancy Huston, co-owner of the Midnight Sun Gallery in Whitehorse, Yukon, says she'll present $20,000 Canadian to the trust fund set up on behalf of Peter Zimmermann at the banquet in February for the Yukon Quest International Sled Dog Race. That would boost the total raised to settle Zimmermann's bills to more than $90,000 in Canadian currency ($58,700 U.S.). For the moment, Zimmerman still owes Canadian hospita…

title: Whaling disaster revisited
summary: Whaling disaster revisited Teams pinpoint 1871 wreckage in Chukchi Sea By MAUREEN CLARK The Associated Press Scientists may have found remnants of one of the greatest shipping calamities in history, buried deep in the muck and mud on the floor of the Chukchi Sea off the northwestern coast of Alaska. The murky images recorded by a remotely operated camera during a late-summer expedition show the outlines of at least two ships that sank more than a century ago. Their bows, decks and the ribs of their hulls are just barely discernible beneath a thick layer of silt, 60 feet below the surface. It's not the Titanic and there are no riches. But the find is thought to represent part of the world's finest collection of 19th century whaling ships. And the discovery may advance the cause of science. …
